item.content:encoded: Another milestone! This article marks the beginning of my fondness for short articles and the inception of Lambda-12. I’m pleased to see it get its due.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Just the thought of a human with smaller humans inside it either constantly breathing, thinking, and passing blood to each other is enough for an upvote.
And then the I read the addendum and had a terrible mental image form up in my head.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This actually explains so many questions I had about Cells at Work!.

item.title: Audio Adaptation
item.content:encoded:
The addendum made me think: what if our world is just the cells of a larger world? Woah.

Anyways, here's wonderwall: https://youtu.be/pkGOgamqgL8

item.title: Oh shit +100
item.content:encoded: In celebration of my second SCP to reach +100 upvotes, I made an addition to the Containment Procedures along those lines.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Why don't the containment procedures say anything about what to do with infected organisms? That seems like it would be a great place to hint at the secondary effect- something like "SCP-2810-1 instances are not to be allowed contact with other instances of the same species"…

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
So cyriak has just posted this. It's an example of how this SCP works.

Also, cute bunnies.

But, cyriak.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I really swore I commented on this when it was posted, but regardless: I really love this article. It builds on an interesting idea to the point that the final addendum hits you suddenly, and you realize the true implication to the whole business. Probably my favorite thing to come from the Short Works Contest.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: No cure? Just expose them to SCP-427 or give them SCP-500.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Ah, Rei Ayanami syndrome.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Hmm - makes me think of some of the cornier animated educational films I saw as a child.

item.title: My Two Cents
item.content:encoded: While most of the skip is good, the line about "traditional insect-repellent measures" makes me imagine the MTF trying to kill the giant mosquito with a can of Raid.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I like the image of a giant flaming mosquito made of normal sized mosquitoes, and I like the implication that other organisms could also join together to form larger versions of themselves.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Neutral voting this one. I think the core idea is an interesting one, however this is a case where the short form constrains the execution a little bit. There are two main pieces of fridge horror/interest here; the giant organism thing, which you hint at above, and the plight of the individual cell-beings that make up the larger organism. I suspect because of space issues, the latter issue is handwaved away by stating that we cannot communicate with mini-humans. But what would it be like, to be a fully-functioning human crammed into a pitch black body temperature flesh cave, desperately trying to replicate a cell's function? I feel like this could have been used to help deepen the narrative.

It's not a bad article and the idea is intriguing, but I feel like there's some missed opportunity here.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Mosquitos aren't carnivorous. Why would a giant mosquito be preying on zebras and not looking for large flowers? If it is a female looking for blood then zebras are too small for the scale (and zebras are already hosts for ectoparasitic females of normal size).

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
All right. It does seem that I missed a lot! Superorganism, flamethrower, bioweapon, supercommunism…
Perhaps this skip would work better out of the constraint of the contest? You are trying to convey a lot of important details here!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
it says nowhere that the infected organism grows in size, so why the 10-meter wingspan? And if all 2810-1 instances are in poor health, how can the giant mosquito hunt zebras?

I think you misunderstood the gigantic mosquito. The idea isn't that the giant mosquito itself is an instance of SCP-2810-1, it's that the giant mosquito is actually composed of normal-sized mosquitoes which themselves are instances of SCP-2810-1.

+ The implication
-

is supposed to be that enough infected organisms can combine into a superorganism similarly to their composite organisms.


As far as the hunt goes, it's never stated that the mosquito was successfully preying on the zebras.

Incidentally: why doesn't insect repellent work on an insect composed of millions of tiny (?) insects?

The "tiny" insects are actually normal-sized mosquitoes. If you have millions of mosquitoes acting as one gigantic mosquito, then for all intents and purposes, it is a gigantic mosquito. The amount of insect repellant they use is simply not enough for one gigantic mosquito.

And how is it possible to analyze a mosquito that's been burned with flamethrowers?

Because we are considering this as a gigantic mosquito, we consider that enough of the mosquito was burned so that it died, but not enough so that it was entirely consumed by flames. Enough normal-sized mosquitoes survived the flames to be analyzed but not enough to sustain the giant mosquito.

In-universe, what is the purpose of this disease/mutation?

This is a bit of a tricky one. The current article itself doesn't give an explanation. However, older revisions tried to hint at a bioweapon, which is retained somewhat in the country of origin (Siberia) and the line "animals become more cooperative and social". The original idea is that it was a failed Russian bioweapon to literally convert all living things to communism.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Oh, mama…. I must be slow, because it just clicked…

+ open
- close

"Animal instances also become much more cooperative and social with each other…"

The disease works both ways!

Hard upvote. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: We don't talk about that Goddamn thing.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I think you're missing the larger question: are you a toaster?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The tiny fractal cells in a swarm of mosquitoes Voltron'd up into a mega mosquito.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Is a reference to a site in-joke.

Also, what if bees are made of larger bees?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Thank you for the image of the 10-meter-wingspan mosquito, A Random Day… ;>

Mmh. Mmmh mmmh. <scratches head> Mmmmmh.
I don't know about this one. I'm with Scantron: I don't buy the biology… But then, I'm REALLY not into biology, and do SCPs have to be plausible? The answer is probably a very firm 'no'!
It seems to me that the article is sometimes not coherent with itself. Example: it says nowhere that the infected organism grows in size, so why the 10-meter wingspan? And if all 2810-1 instances are in poor health, how can the giant mosquito hunt zebras?
Incidentally: why doesn't insect repellent work on an insect composed of millions of tiny (?) insects?
And how is it possible to analyze a mosquito that's been burned with flamethrowers?
In-universe, what is the purpose of this disease/mutation? Poor mental/physical health: not an improvement. What does the pathogen get out of the infection?

Mmh mmh mmh. Mmh. Downvote. (I am determined not to neutral-vote on SC2015s)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This is dumb and cute and I really like it, enough to upvote. That said, I'm not sure the final addendum adds much, plus

Infected plants produce very little fruit, and produce poor facsimiles of their true fruit.

Is kind of awkwardly worded.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I don't find the biology plausible. Sorry.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Talk about turtles all the way down…

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The whole article reminds me this.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Yeah, this is pretty cool. I'll be honest, I wasn't too confident in this contest, but this is one of the three or four I actually really like. +1.

EDIT: Just saw the name on the wiki - I personally don't like it, and I doubt you're willing to chand it, but if you do, I would definitely recommend "It's A Cellular Life". xD

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
If the mosquitoes composing a normal mosquito were all cell sized then wouldn't a mosquito whose cells were the size of mosquitoes be a lot bigger than 1m? Whatever, I like this a lot.

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Simple, yet very effective. A pathogen that turns whatever it infects into living fractals… intriguing yet squicky in more ways than one. Take my upvote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Thank you for giving me yet another mind worm… mind putting a memetic hazard warning up there? Now all I can think about is bees infected with this SCP…

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Well, in its original draft (if I recall), it did have an origin of sorts. Since it's not brought up in the final product, I am really curious what is it.

Currently, the article definitely shows. But in the realm of explaining, it's less emphasised (perhaps it's better that way). At least I am treated to a giant mosquito.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Does there have to be a point? I find that the imagery is what makes this article, I don't think there needs to be an origin story. It's entirely possible this is just a natural phenomenon, an entry doesn't need a boring shoed-in backstory to succeed.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Very interesting premise. I hate to see what DNA is made up of. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Pros:

  • The imagery of those infected by 2810 is something worth imagining about. It leaves it to respective readers to think of it, and boy are the images… just weird!
  • You actually caught me by surprise with the addendum there, which leaves quite eerie implications (like how it wound up in Africa when it originated from Siberia, or how does it affect animals and plants).

Cons:

  • I still wonder what's the whole point behind this disease. Is it manmade? If so, for what purpose?

Verdict: Upvote

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Gah, the mental image of this one makes me giggle and recoil in disgust simultaneously. Although this begs the question: what if bees were made of smaller of bees?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This is the culmination of twelve days of frantically pumping out pages between the hours of 11 PM and 8 AM depending on where I could get sleep and where my neck would break if I tried. It's also the end product of brainstorming sessions that ended up in squid rape and a lost Arabian city.

SO MANY THANKS to psul, who basically helped me turn this into what it is. Also thanks to MrWrong, Djoric, Waterfire, EldritchCyanide, and BrandonBriggs for their last-minute feedback.

See more of my work on my author page!
